description:
This artwork of six dolls is symbolic of the duality of human persona. There is an intrinsic and extrinsic part in all of us. What we appear may not be as to what we are in our personality. An angry looking man might be really soft hearted and vice versa. The masks that are seen in front of the wooden figures are their inner embodiment. When the masks are removed by pulling the lever, their outer semblance is revealed. 

Rabin Roy completed his B.V.A. from govt. college of Art & Craft, Calcutta in 1997. He has many stellar Durga Puja Pandals to his credit, now recognised by UNESCO. He also has major solo shows to his credit namely Bharat Bhavan (Bhopal, 2015), Akar Prakar Gallery, Kolkata, 2009, Temline Kunstverein, Germany (2001) and many workshops as well. (Kolkata, Delhi, Gangtok)

The two variations of ‘Shiv Parvati’ on horse is symbiotic of Paper Mash, Clay & Colour. Papier-mâché, which means “mashed paper” in French, arrived in Kashmir during the 14th and 15th centuries and the persian mystic Mir Sayyid Ali Hamadani introduced the art, and King Zain-ul-Abidinalso known as the Akbar of Kashmir, helped it flourish.Decorative panels for prayer rooms, miniature shrines, and festival decorations have made papier-mâché an integral part of religious life. The lightweight nature makes these items perfect for temporary installations during festivals.

The two variations of ‘Shiv Parvati’ on horse and bullock is made by artisans from Andhra Pradesh, and plays with colours and form akin to the temples of the region. Papier mache is a versatile, eco-friendly folk artparticularly renowned in Kashmir and Bihar, India, for creating durable, lightweight decorative items from recycled paper pulp. It involves moulding pulp with binders, polishing with stones, and intricate, vibrant hand-painting, frequently depicting nature-inspired, geometric, or mythological themes.

The King & Queen chair is a made of wood and the intricateand beautiful pieces reflects the rich heritage of Odisha's traditional art forms.
  
The pieces have been crafted by Guru Shree Bibhu Maharana, a renowned National Award-winning Pattachitra artist from the traditional crafts village of Danda Sahi, near Puri, Odisha. He specializes in intricate, traditional Pattachitra paintings (cloth-based) and palm leaf etchings, as well as woodwork. He is trying to keep alive the traditional Gurukul system of learning and propagating the art form and strongly believes Pattachitra is a divine art form and it brings him closer to himself, and the Lord of the Universe, Lord Jagannath.

Cowbells are primarily used by farmers to track, locate, and ensure the safety of free-roaming livestock (especially cows) grazing in expansive or hilly areas, allowing them to be heard over long distances. They also serve as a way to identify cows in a herd. Interestingly, cowbells are something of a signature craft in India. Forged from metal and scraps, the Indian cowbell is not fancy or frivolous. It is a simple, practical object that delights with its unique sound and handmade look. But more than being charming, Indian cowbells carry with them a legacy of traditional handicraft.

Bells come in a variety of sizes, and expert bell makers are able to tune their wares with fine precision. In the past, bells could be tuned to have a specific sound at the request of the herder. By asking for a certain sound, the herder would know how to distinguish his cattle from another’s.
